coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photoelectric performance of TiO2 nanotubes sensitized with carbon dots derived from bagasse
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Qian
Lin, Gui
Meng, Lili
Zhou, Liya
Hu, Lijia
Nong, Jingfeng
Li, Yupo
Wang, Jiayu
Hu, Kelin
Yu, Qiongguang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with tuneable bandgap were prepared via a hydrothermal method using bagasse derived from industrial waste as carbon source. The CQDs exhibit a visible range absorption with excitation-dependent fluorescence emission. Using a liquid electrolyte, the photovoltaic conversion efficiency of prepared the CQDs/TiO2 nanotubes (TNTAs) reached 1.44% with a sensitization time of 6.0 h and sensitization temperature of 100 °C, which was 13.09 times that of TNTAs. This study demonstrates the fabrication of low-cost and efficient quantum dots sensitized solar cells with a liquid electrolyte using CQDs from bagasse.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Quantum dots
TiO2 nanotube
Solar cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of TNTAs sensitized with CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The XRD patterns of CQDs. (B) The XRD patterns of TNTAs, CQDs/TNTAs for different times.
The UV–vis absorption (black) and PL spectra excited at different wavelengths of CQDs.

The top view SEM images of TNTAs (A), CQDs/TNTAs for sensitization times of 2.0 h (B), 4.0 h (C), 6.0 h (D), and 8.0 h (E). The EDS spectrum of TNTAs (F) and CQDs/TNTAs (I). The elements mapping of O (G) and Ti (H) in TNTAs, and the elements mapping of C (J), O (K), and Ti (L) in CQDs/TNTAs.
(A) The mechanism of QDSSCs. (B) Plots of (αhν)2 versus hν for the CQDs with different hydrothermal temperature (inset: UV–vis absorption for the CQDs with different hydrothermal temperature).

(A) The J–V characteristics for bare TNTAs and CQDs/TNTAs at 100 °C with different sensitization time. (B) The J–V characteristics for bare TNTAs and CQDs/TNTAs at 6.0 h with different sensitization temperature.
Photovoltaic parameters for bare TNTAs and CQDs/TNTAs at 100 °C with different sensitization time.
Photovoltaic parameters for bare TNTAs and CQDs/TNTAs at 6.0 h with different sensitization temperature.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photoelectric performance of TiO2 nanotubes sensitized with carbon dots derived from bagasse

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Non-Food Biomass and Enzyme Technology, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Non-Food Biomass and Enzyme Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Non-Food Biomass and Enzyme Technology, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with tuneable bandgap were prepared via a hydrothermal method using bagasse derived from industrial waste as carbon source. The CQDs exhibit a visible range absorption with excitation-dependent fluorescence emission. Using a liquid electrolyte, the photovoltaic conversion efficiency of prepared the CQDs/TiO2 nanotubes (TNTAs) reached 1.44% with a sensitization time of 6.0 h and sensitization temperature of 100 °C, which was 13.09 times that of TNTAs. This study demonstrates the fabrication of low-cost and efficient quantum dots sensitized solar cells with a liquid electrolyte using CQDs from bagasse.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs) have licited growing interest due to the increasingly serious energy crisis and environmental problems. TiO2 nanotubes (TNTAs) is one of the most promising semiconductor materials used in QDSSCs given its low price, environment-friendly characteristic and satisfactory photovoltaic (PV) performance [1–3]. However, visible light cannot be effectively absorbed by pure TNTAs because of the large bandgap (3.2 eV) [4]. The surface sensitization of TNTAs using narrow bandgap semiconductors is the most effective method for improving the absorption of the solar spectrum [1–7].

Compared with conventional quantum dots, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are characterised by low cytotoxicity, chemical inertness, cost effectiveness, and excellent biocompatibility [8]. To date, various of materials have been proposed to prepare CQDs since CQDs were first reported in 2004 [9–13]. The CQDs prepared using sucrose molasses exhibit good photostability and fluorescence properties with a particle size of 1.9 nm [11]. Based on the excellent optical properties, the CQDs have been used as the sensitizer in QDSSCs. The CQDs were introduced into TiO2 as a light adsorption layer to increase photoconversion efficiency (PCE) to 0.13%. Meanwhile, the PCE of bare TiO2 was 0.03% [12]. Water-soluble and oil-soluble CQDs were prepared via one-step synthesis using pure citric acid as carbon source, and the PCE of the QDSSCs using the two prepared CQDs as sensitizers reached 0.22% and 0.15%, respectively [13].

Bagasse, a type of wood fiber, is also the largest by-product of sugar production. With an annual bagasse production reaching millions of tons, the natural environment and the sugar industry are facing heavy burden and considerable challenge. Therefore, the exploration of bagasse application must be encouraged. In this study, the pre-synthesized CQDs using bagasse as materials were deposited onto TNTAs. The optical properties and morphology of the prepared CQDs were investigated. The PV performance of the CQDs/TNTAs solar cells with liquid electrolyte was study.

The CQDs were hydrothermal synthesized by using bagasse as precursors. Typically, 5.0 g of bagasse was added to an autoclave and heated at different temperatures for 12 h to control the optical properties of the CQDs. Subsequently, 30 mL of deionized water was added to the mixture after cooling to room temperature. The resulting solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove large particles.

Highly ordered TNTAs were synthesised via electrochemical anodization using Ti foils as anode [2]. Subsequently, acetone, ethanol and deionized water were used for the ultrasonic washing of Ti foils for 15 min. The cleaned Ti foils were anodized at room temperature in a NH4F electrolyte at 45 V for 8.0 h, and then annealed for 3.0 h at 450 °C in air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of TNTAs sensitized with CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs/TNTAs were prepared using the direct adsorption (DA) method. The TNTAs were placed in a petri dish with 30 mL of CQDs. Then, the petri dish was placed in an oven for sensitization at different times and temperatures.

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on a Rigaku D/max-IIIA X-ray diffractometer. The morphology and energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS) of the samples were recorded on a Quanta 250 scanning electron microscope coupled with an energy-dispersive spectrometer. The Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained using an Agilent Cary-100 UV–Vis spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. A three-electrode configuration was used to measure the PCE of the samples with a Pt counter electrode and a Ag/AgCl reference electrode in an electrolyte containing 0.7 M Na2SO3 and 0.5 M Na2S. The PCE parameters were calculated from the current density–voltage (J–V) characteristic, which was measured using a computer-programmed Keithley 2400 Source Meter under 100 mW/cm2 irradiation with Zolix SS150 solar simulation.

Fig. 1 A shows the XRD patterns of the CQDs. The typical broad diffraction peaks are observed at approximately 24°, confirming the incomplete and discontinuous amorphous carbon structure of the prepared CQDs [14]. The XRD patterns of the bare TNTAs, and the CQDs/TNTAs sensitized for different time are shown in Fig. 1B. The three characteristic peaks at approximately 25.42°, 38.20°, and 70.90°corresponded to the reflection from the (1 0 1), (0 0 4), and (1 1 6) planes of the anatase phase of TiO2, respectively (JCPDS No. 21–1272). No characteristic peaks were observed for phases other than that of the bare TNTAs in the XRD patterns of the CQDs/TNTAs. This observation suggests that the existence of CQDs has no effect on the TNTAs lattice structure. Furthermore, the characteristic peak of CQDs can not be observed due to the strong signals of the TNTAs crystal [11].

The CQDs are known for their optical properties. The UV–Vis spectra of the prepared CQDs were studied (Fig. 2 ). Two typical absorption peaks at approximately 280 nm and 360 nm were observed in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum. These peaks are attributed to the π–π* transition of the C = C group and the n–π* transition of the multi-conjugate C = C of the CQDs, respectively [15,16]. Moreover, the prepared CQDs were absorbed in the UV and visible regions. The redshift of the emission wavelength was observed when varying the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 500 nm, suggesting that the photoluminescence (PL) wavelength of CQDs is a function of excitation wavelength [17]. Moreover, emission intensity increased and then decreased as the excitation wavelength changed from 300 nm to 500 nm, and the maximum intensity was observed at 450 nm upon excitation at 360 nm. The mechanism of the excitation-dependent PL properties may result from emissive traps on the surfaces and the size distribution of CQDs [11].

Fig. 3 (A-E) show the top-view scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the TNTAs and the CQDs/TNTAs with different deposition times. The TNTAs structures were uniform over a wide area, with an internal tube diameter and thickness of approximately 120 nm and 40 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 (B-E), CQDs were deposited successfully on the TNTAs surfaces, and the deposition of CQDs increased with the duration of sensitization time, whilst the TNTAs structure was retained. A large number of CQDs nearly covered the entire surface of TNTAs when the sensitization time was 8.0 h.

The EDS results (Fig. 3F-H) show that the TNTAs are composed of the elements Ti and O with uniform distribution. The evident signals in Fig. 3I indicate that the CQDs/TNTAs consist of the elements C, Ti, and O. Besides, the C, Ti, and O elements, which are evenly distributed on the CQDs/TNTAs, suggesting that the CQDs have been introduced into the TNTAs successfully.

Fig. 4 A describes the mechanism of QDSSCs. Upon irradiation by sunlight, the QDs absorbed energy, and electron-holes pairs were prepared. The photogenerated electrons transitioned from the valence band (VB) of the QDs to their conduction band (CB). Due to the good matching of energy levels, electrons transferred from the CB of the QDs to the CB of TNTAs and then to the external circuit, whilst the oxidized QDs were reduced with the electrolyte. Therefore, the key component related to the photoelectric performance of QDSSCs is the light absorption capacity of QDs. QDs with a smaller bandgap can achieve a better photoelectric conversion efficiency due to their better sunlight utilization. According to inset of Fig. 4B, the absorption intensity increased when the hydrothermal temperature increased from 150 °C to 200 °C. As the hydrothermal temperature was further increased beyond 200 °C, the absorption intensity decreased. To study the bandgap of obtained QDs, the (αhν)2 versus hν curses of the CQDs with different temperatures for 12 h are shown in Fig. 4B. The value of the optical bandgap can be calculated using the classical Eq. (1), as follows [18]:
(1)
α h ν = A
( h ν -
E g
)
n
where, α is the absorption constant, hν is the photon energy, A is the constant, and Eg is the bandgap. When n = 1/2, the corresponding direct bandgap was provided by extrapolating the linear region of these graphs to (αhν)2 = 0. The direct bandgap for the samples decreased from 2.66 eV to 1.95 eV when temperature was increased from 150 °C to 200 °C. The direct bandgap for the CQDs increased with a further increased temperature. The results show that the bandgap energy of the CQDs can be adjusted by adjusting the temperature. Moreover, the corresponding bandgap of the CQDs synthesized at 200 °C was the smallest, effectively improving the photoelectric conversion efficiency of the prepared photoanodes.

The J–V curves of the CQDs/TNTAs photoanodes were examined to study the PV performance of the prepared photoanodes. In the J–V measurement process, the fill factor (FF) was calculated using Eq. (2), and the PCE was calculated using Eq. (3).
(2)
FF =
J × V
max
/
J sc
×
V oc
,
(3)
P C E
%
=
FF ×
J sc
×
V oc
×
100
/
P light
,
where the maximum product of current density (J) and current voltage (V), short-circuit current density (J sc), and open-circuit voltage (V oc) can be calculated from the J–V curve. The P light is the incident optical power. The influences of the sensitization time and temperature were investigated. Fig. 5 A shows the J–V curves for the bare TNTAs and the CQDs/TNTAs sensitized at 100 °C for 2.0 h, 4.0 h, 6.0 h, and 8.0 h. The corresponding PV parameters are provided in Table 1 . A poor PCE of 0.11% was achieved by the TNTAs with a J sc of 3.09 mA/cm2, a V oc of 126 mV, and a FF of 0.29. The PCE increased with increasing sensitization time from 2.0 h to 6.0 h. The highest PCE value was 1.44% at a sensitization time of 6.0 h. The equivalent J sc was 6.35 mA/cm2 and the V oc was 635 mV. However, when the sensitization time increased to 8.0 h, the excessive deposition of CQDs decreased PCE, leading to the aggregation and growth of CQDs, and consequently the increase in recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the decrease in output [2]. The results show that the optimum sensitization time is 6.0 h.

The J–V characteristics and corresponding PV parameters of the bare TNTAs and the CQDs/TNTAs with different sensitization temperature for 6.0 h are presented in Fig. 5B and the Table 2 , respectively. The PCE of the CQDs/TNTAs also increased with an increase in temperature from 50 °C to 100 °C, and then the PCE decreased as temperature increased. This phenomenon probably is caused by the easy agglomeration of CQDs at high temperatures [19]. The PCE of the CQDs/TNTAs can reach a maximum of 1.44% with a liquid electrolyte. Such value shows the better PV performance compared with the PCE of TNTAs.

The CQDs derived from bagasse were successfully fabricated using a hydrothermal method. The UV–Vis adsorption range of prepared CQDs was mainly in the UV and visible region. The direct bandgap of the CQDs could be modified by controlling hydrothermal temperature. The CQDs with an incomplete and discontinuous amorphous structure emitted excitation-dependent PL spectra. The synthesised CQDs were successively sensitized on the TNTAs surface using the DA method. The PCE of the CQDs/TNTAs reached a maximum of 1.44% with a sensitization time of 6.0 h and a sensitization temperature of 100 °C. This valve is 13.09 times that of pure TNTAs (0.11%). The outcomes demonstrate the potential application of CQDs from bagasse to QDSSCs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No 51762008); the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Non-Food Biomass and Enzyme Technology (2018-15-Z03-06); the Basic ability improvement project for young teachers in Guangxi colleges and universities (2019KY0397); the Chemistry Doctorate Construction of Nanning Normal University; the Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Nanning Normal University.
